revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Iwayayama Kofun]][[ja:岩屋山古墳]][[zh:岩屋山古墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|zh|方墳|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} |name=Iwayayama Kofun |image=File:Iwayayama Kofun, senmon.jpg |location={{ill|Asuka|qid=Q752397|1=Asuka, Nara}} |coordinates={{Coord|34.46572222|N|135.79763889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |type={{ill|Square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (or {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}?) |area=Approximately 45 meters on each side |Constructed=Mid to 3rd quarter of the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|qid=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}} |burial=Bilateral {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} |burial= |burial goods = |Historic site =National {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|qid=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan|1=Historic site}}} "Iwayayama Kofun" |notes= }} '''Iwayayama Kofun''' (岩屋山古墳) is a {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|qid=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} located in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|qid=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara|1=Asuka, Nara}}, {{ill|Takaichi District, Nara|en|Takaichi District, Nara|qid=Q1117901|lt=Takaichi District, Nara|1=Takaichi District, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}. It has a {{ill|square Kofun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} structure and is Historic site as a national {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|qid=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan|1=Historic site}}}.
== Overview == The existence of Iwayayama Kofun has been widely known since ancient times due to its exposed corridor-style stone chamber. It is believed to be a square Kofun with sides approximately 45 meters long, although some theories suggest it may have been an octagonal Kofun. In 1978, severe water damage caused the west side of the mound, which had already been eroded, to collapse. This led to environmental conservation efforts and an archaeological excavation.
=== Mound === The mound, originally built in two tiers, was revealed through excavations to have measured about 45 meters on each side and approximately 12 meters in height. The mound was constructed with carefully layered {{ill|Rammed earth|en|Rammed earth|qid=Q1363009|lt=rammed earth|1=Rammed earth}}. While the lower tier is distinctly square, some scholars, such as Taichiro Shiraishi, have suggested that the upper tier may have been octagonal. This hypothesis is based on a survey map analysis. Octagonal mounds are characteristic of royal tombs from the Asuka period, such as the {{ill|Goryōno Kofun|ja|御廟野古墳|1=Gobyō-no-Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Goryōno Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Gobyō-no-Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (attributed to Emperor Tenji) and the {{ill|Noguchi-ōsaka Kofun|ja|野口王墓古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Noguchi-ōsaka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 野口王墓}} (joint tomb of Emperor Tenmu and Empress Jitō).
=== Stone Chamber === [[File:Iwayayama Kofun, 3D-B-2.jpg|thumb|300px|right|{{center|Overhead view of the stone chamber}}]] [[File:Iwayayama Kofun, 3D-N-2.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|Unfolded view of the stone chamber}}]] The stone chamber, built entirely of granite, is a bilateral {{ill|corridor-style stone chamber|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/corridor-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} with precision-cut stones lining its walls. The burial chamber features a two-tier construction, with a back wall made of two large slabs and side walls composed of five stones (two in the upper tier and three in the lower tier). The walls incline inward at the top, and the ceiling consists of a single massive slab<ref>'Japanese Ancient Ruins Encyclopedia,' p. 567.</ref>. The corridor walls are one-tiered in the rear section and two-tiered in the front section.
The burial chamber measures 4.9 meters in length, 2.7 meters in width, and 3 meters in height, while the corridor measures 12 meters in length and 1.9 meters in width. Several kofun in Nara Prefecture and southern Osaka exhibit the same design and specifications as Iwayayama Kofun, including the Muneshika No. 1 Kofun in Sakurai and smaller variations like the Kotani Kofun in Kashihara and the Minezuka Kofun in Tenri. Additionally, the {{ill|Eifukuji North Kofun|ja|叡福寺北古墳|1=Eifuku-ji North Kofun|2=ja|3=叡福寺北古墳|4=fr|5=Eifukuji Kita Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eifukuji North Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Eifuku-ji North Kofun|qq=}} (traditionally considered the tomb of Prince Shōtoku) is believed to share this style<ref name="#1">Sumiyuki Habu, "Kofun," pp. 184–186.</ref>. Shiraishi refers to this style as the "Iwayayama style," which represents a key example of late-period corridor-style stone chambers. Later adaptations, such as the simpler "Iwayayama sub-style," can be seen in the Kusabuka Kofun and Nishinomiya Kofun in Nara Prefecture<ref>Shiraishi (1982), pp. 95–97.</ref>.
Excavations revealed that the chamber had been looted, and a secondary burial, likely from the medieval period, was found in the center of the burial chamber<ref>Itō (1984), pp. 168–169.</ref>. No burial goods from the original burial were recovered.
=== built and burial === Shiraishi dates the construction of Iwayayama Kofun and similar Iwayayama-style stone chambers to around the mid-7th century to its third quarter. He also suggests the possibility that this kofun may have been the tomb of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Empress Saimei|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}, who was buried at Ochioka-no-e-no-Misasagi in 667 AD (Tenji 6)<ref>Shiraishi (1982), pp. 99–dd1.</ref>.
However, alternative theories argue that the Iwayayama style's construction period may date back to the early 7th century. These theories consider the associated burial dates of Prince Shōtoku (621–622 AD) at the Eifukuji North Kofun and Prince Kurame (603 AD) at the Tsukiana Kofun in Osaka. These theories suggest an earlier timeframe for Iwayayama-style tombs<ref>Kishimoto (20dd), pp. 80–88.</ref>. Further research and archaeological discoveries are required to clarify these hypotheses.
== Historic site == === National Historic site === * Iwayayama Kofun - Designated on May dd, 1968.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Sources == * Taichiro Shiraishi, "The End of Kofuns in Kinai," National Museum of Japanese History Research Report Vol. 1, 1982, pp. 79–120. * Yūsuke Itō, "Iwayayama Kofun," in 'Talking about the Kofuns of Yamato,' Rokko Publishing, 1984, pp. 168–169. * Sumiyuki Habu, 'Kofuns' (Historical and Cultural Library), Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 20dd, pp. 184–186. * Hatsushige Ōtsuka, Kiyohiko Sakurai, Kimio Suzuki (Eds.), 'Japanese Ancient Ruins Encyclopedia,' Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 1995, p. 567. * Naofumi Kishimoto, "The burial of Corridor-Style Stone Chambers Reflects the Activity builts of Interred_individuals," 'Archaeological Research,' Vol. 58, No. 1, Archaeological Research Society, 20dd, pp. 78–89.
== External Links == {{Commonscat|Iwayayama_Kofun}} * {{National Historic site Database|401|2012|Iwayayama Kofun}} * [http://www.city.kashihara.nara.jp/kankou/own_sekaiisan/sekaiisan/kouseiisan/iwayayama.html Kashihara Exploration Guide (Official Kashihara City Website)] {{Dead link|date=October 2017 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }}
{{Kofun navbar}}
